critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression readmission prediction experiment is a near-duplicate of eval_scenario_786 and eval_scenario_791 structurally, and it shares their core problems. The stratified random split is justified by the claim that 'temporal autocorrelation in patient outcomes is not significant enough to necessitate a purely chronological split' — but this is asserted without evidence for a three-year, 100,000-record EHR dataset. Hospital admission rates, treatment protocols, and readmission patterns all exhibit year-over-year and seasonal variation. Random splitting allows future-period patterns to inform training.

Recall is again selected as the primary metric to capture high-risk patients under class imbalance — but optimizing for recall alone encourages the model to predict readmission for all patients (trivially achieving recall = 1.0). The justification for recall as primary is stated but the complementary constraint on precision or a minimum precision threshold is absent. PR-AUC would be a more appropriate primary metric that captures the precision-recall tradeoff.

The feature asymmetry issue is present: the RNN uses 'time-stamped sequences of diagnosis codes, treatment history, and follow-up visits' while logistic regression uses 'aggregate demographic and clinical variables.' Again, architecture and feature richness are confounded. An intermediate baseline — logistic regression with sequence-derived aggregate features (number of prior admissions, days since last visit, most recent lab trends) — would help isolate the contribution of sequential modeling.

The design states 'data pipelines are inspected for potential pipeline leakages or biases' but provides no specifics. For EHR data where discharge summary dates, lab result availability, and imaging report timestamps all have complex availability windows, this assertion without a concrete lookback window policy is insufficient.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split assumption of weak temporal autocorrelation is asserted without evidence for multi-year EHR data where seasonal and policy-driven admission patterns are well-documented.
Recall-only primary metric incentivizes trivial maximization by predicting all patients as high-risk; PR-AUC or recall at specified precision constraints is more appropriate.
Feature set asymmetry between RNN (time-stamped sequences) and logistic regression (demographic aggregates) confounds architecture with feature richness.
The leakage inspection claim lacks specificity — complex EHR timestamp availability (lab results, imaging reports) requires explicit lookback window definitions.
Patient-level split isolation is not mentioned — multiple admissions per patient distributed across train/test constitute an unaddressed leakage risk.
Recall as primary metric without precision constraint or AUCPR allows trivial high-recall models to win model selection
Temporal stationarity assumed but not empirically tested over the three-year EHR window
Feature asymmetry between RNN (temporal sequences) and logistic regression (aggregates) confounds architecture advantage with feature richness
Patient-level containment in train/test splits is unaddressed for multi-admission patients
No calibration assessment despite the clinical application requiring well-calibrated risk probabilities for intervention prioritization

Stratified random split on three-year patient EHR data is not justified by the asserted assumption of temporal stationarity; chronological splitting with patient-level blocking is required for prospective validity
Recall as primary metric without a precision constraint or specified operating point enables degenerate all-positive classifiers to score optimally during model selection

Patient-level grouping is absent — patients with multiple hospital encounters over three years will appear in both training and test sets under random splitting, inflating performance through direct patient-level leakage

Follow-up visit data in RNN feature sequences must be verified to exclude any visits that are part of the readmission trajectory — post-discharge follow-up visits leading to readmission would constitute target leakage
No calibration evaluation specified; clinical readmission risk stratification requires well-calibrated probability outputs for threshold-based clinical decision-making
